content.text: A blameless postmortem examines what happened at the technical level. The major incident executive review examines what the incident means at the business level: how it affected customers, what the financial and reputational exposure was, whether action items are adequately resourced, and what policy or process changes are warranted at an organisational level. The two processes are complementary, not redundant — the exec review consumes the postmortem output but operates on a different scope and with a different audience.

The executive review exists because some decisions cannot be made by the engineering team alone. Re-prioritising a reliability investment over a feature roadmap, approving a customer SLA credit, changing the on-call policy, or committing to a public post-incident report all require executive authority. Without a structured review process, those decisions get deferred or made inconsistently.

attrs.alt: Exec review escalation path: Trigger Conditions (any P0, P1 with >4h MTTR, P1 with >500K users, repeat failure in 90 days) → Gate (postmortem complete, action items assigned) → Exec Review Meeting (within 2 weeks, 60 min, VP Eng/Product/Legal/Comms/CTO) → Outcomes (escalated action items, policy changes, customer comms approved, decision record). 60-minute agenda: 0-10 min impact summary, 10-25 min timeline and root cause, 25-40 min action items, 40-55 min resource decisions, 55-60 min decision record sign-off.

content.text: The exec review triggers automatically for specific incident categories. Automating the trigger removes the judgment call about whether an incident is "serious enough" — that subjectivity leads to reviews being skipped precisely when they are most needed.
VP Engineering, VP Product, Head of Comms
Second P0 or P1 within 90 days
CTO, VP Engineering, EM of affected team
Any incident involving user data or model safety

The gate before scheduling the review is that the postmortem must be complete and action items must be assigned to owners with due dates. Scheduling a review before the postmortem is finalised produces an unproductive meeting — executives cannot make resource decisions about action items that have not yet been identified.

The engineering team prepares a review package 48 hours before the meeting. The package is distinct from the technical postmortem: it is written for an audience that does not have the technical context to interpret raw metrics or code-level root cause analysis. Every technical detail must be translated into business impact.
What failed, when, for how long, how many users were affected, and what the financial exposure is. Written at Director-level reading comprehension — no acronyms, no infrastructure jargon.

Five to eight key events from the incident — first symptom detected, customer reports received, mitigation deployed, full resolution. The full technical timeline lives in the postmortem; the exec version shows only decision points.

Number of affected users, API request failure count, estimated revenue impact, and SLA exposure (credits owed). Segment by customer tier if relevant — a P0 that affected only free-tier users has different business implications than one that affected enterprise customers.
All action items from the postmortem, with owner, due date, category (detection / prevention / runbook / process), and whether any require resources beyond the current sprint allocation.

An explicit list of the decisions the team needs executives to make at the meeting — resource approval, customer communication sign-off, policy changes, or escalation of cross-team blockers. Executives should know before they enter the room what they are being asked to decide.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import datetime, date
from decimal import Decimal
from enum import Enum

class DecisionType(str, Enum):
    RESOURCE_APPROVAL       = "resource_approval"
    CUSTOMER_COMMS_SIGN_OFF = "customer_comms_sign_off"
    POLICY_CHANGE           = "policy_change"
    CROSS_TEAM_ESCALATION   = "cross_team_escalation"
    SLA_CREDIT_APPROVAL     = "sla_credit_approval"

@dataclass
class ExecReviewDecision:
    decision_type: DecisionType
    description: str
    options: list[str]
    recommended: str
    urgency_days: int          # must be decided within N days of meeting

@dataclass
class ExecReviewPackage:
    incident_id: str
    severity: str
    resolved_at: datetime
    mttr_minutes: float
    affected_users: int
    api_failures: int
    estimated_revenue_impact_usd: Decimal
    sla_credit_exposure_usd: Decimal
    action_items: list[dict]   # from postmortem
    decisions_requested: list[ExecReviewDecision] = field(default_factory=list)

    def validate(self) -> list[str]:
        errors = []
        if not self.action_items:
            errors.append("action_items must not be empty — postmortem must be complete")
        items_without_owners = [ai for ai in self.action_items if not ai.get("owner")]
        if items_without_owners:
            errors.append(f"{len(items_without_owners)} action items have no owner assigned")
        items_needing_resources = [
            ai for ai in self.action_items if ai.get("needs_resource_approval")
        ]
        resource_decisions = [
            d for d in self.decisions_requested
            if d.decision_type == DecisionType.RESOURCE_APPROVAL
        ]
        if items_needing_resources and not resource_decisions:
            errors.append("action items require resource approval but no RESOURCE_APPROVAL decision is listed")
        return errors

The meeting is 60 minutes and follows a fixed agenda. The Incident Commander or Engineering Manager presents; executives ask questions and make decisions. The meeting is not a debriefing or a blame session — its purpose is to surface decisions and commitments that require executive authority.
Executives aligned on scope and customer exposure
Resource needs surfaced; cross-team blockers escalated
Each decision from the package assigned an owner and due date
Decision record read back and confirmed by attendees

No decision should leave the meeting without an owner and a due date. Decisions that are "discussed but not resolved" degrade the process — the team walks away without the authority it needs to act, and the same conversation repeats at the next review.

The decision record is the binding output of the exec review. It is drafted by the EM during the meeting (in a shared document visible to all attendees) and confirmed before the meeting ends. It is published to the incident management system within 24 hours.

The decision record is read-only once published. If a decision needs to be revisited, a new record entry is created with a reference to the original — this preserves the audit trail and prevents silent revision of what was agreed.
Major incidents involving AI model behaviour require additional steps in the executive review that do not apply to infrastructure-only outages.

Any incident where the model produced outputs that violated safety guidelines requires Policy and Legal to attend the review, and the Responsible Scaling Policy team to be briefed. These are not treated as ordinary P0 incidents — they have their own review track.

If a model deployment caused measurable degradation in output quality (measured by automated evals or user-reported quality), the review must address whether the deployment gate failed and what the rollback criteria should have been. Quality regressions are not purely engineering issues — they have product and trust implications.

If the incident involved potential exposure of user conversations or model inputs, Legal and Privacy must attend, and the incident may trigger mandatory external notification timelines under applicable privacy regulations. The exec review must confirm that notification decisions have been made and documented.

If an incident originated in a third-party model or API dependency (not Anthropic's own models), the review must assess whether the dependency SLA is adequate and whether fallback to an alternative model or cached responses is warranted.
Executives cannot make resource decisions about action items that have not been identified. The gate must be enforced — if the postmortem is late, push the review, not the gate.
Technical briefing instead of executive briefing:

Presenting the raw postmortem to executives produces a 30-minute discussion about what a token error is before the business impact is even reached. The executive package must be authored separately from the technical postmortem.

"We'll follow up on that" is not a decision. Each item in the decisions-requested list must be resolved in the room or explicitly deferred with a named owner and due date for the deferral to be resolved.

Decision records without a follow-up mechanism are theatre. The EM who owns the decision record is responsible for verifying that each decision was executed and for flagging any that missed their due date.
Blame language in the exec package:

Phrases like "the on-call engineer failed to escalate" or "X made the wrong call" in an executive document create legal exposure and undermine psychological safety. The exec package, like the postmortem, is blameless — it describes system conditions, not individual failures.

The Incident Commander or Engineering Manager who presents to executives often has deep technical context but limited practice communicating with a non-technical audience under pressure. A 30-minute pre-brief with the exec sponsor (typically the VP of Engineering) before the full review prevents the most common presentation failures: over-explaining technical details, reacting defensively to questions, and losing the thread of what decisions are needed.
Lead with impact, not with cause:
Open with "X users experienced Y for Z hours, with an estimated $N financial impact" before explaining what failed. Executives need the business context to frame everything that follows.

"Could this happen again?" Every exec review ends with this question, explicitly or implicitly. Have a specific answer: what action item directly addresses recurrence risk, who owns it, and when it will be closed.

Signal clearly when you are stating a measured fact ("the alert fired 14 minutes after the failure") vs. an interpretation ("we believe the alert threshold was too conservative"). Executives sometimes make decisions based on interpretations without realising they are not facts.

If an executive asks a critical question about a decision made during the incident, answer factually about what was known at the time. Defensive responses signal to the room that the culture is not actually blameless, regardless of what the postmortem policy says.

For incidents above a customer-impact threshold, the exec review is the decision point for external disclosures beyond the status page update. These decisions have legal and reputational implications and must not be made unilaterally by the communications or engineering teams.
P0 or P1 with >1M user impact
SLA breach for any enterprise account
Press inquiry or high-profile public outage
As required; Comms drafts, CEO approves

The decision on external disclosures must be recorded in the decision record with the rationale. "We decided not to publish a post-incident report because the impact was below our threshold" is a legitimate decision; the absence of any record of having considered it is not.

The exec review produces a decision record, but the decisions only have value if they are executed. The EM who facilitated the review owns follow-up tracking: verifying that each decision was acted on, escalating any that are stalled, and closing the loop with a brief written status update to all attendees.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import date, timedelta
from enum import Enum

class FollowUpStatus(str, Enum):
    PENDING    = "pending"
    IN_PROGRESS = "in_progress"
    DONE       = "done"
    OVERDUE    = "overdue"
    ESCALATED  = "escalated"

@dataclass
class FollowUpItem:
    decision_id: str
    description: str
    owner: str
    due_date: date
    status: FollowUpStatus = FollowUpStatus.PENDING
    completed_at: date | None = None
    evidence_url: str | None = None

    def evaluate(self, as_of: date | None = None) -> FollowUpStatus:
        today = as_of or date.today()
        if self.status == FollowUpStatus.DONE:
            return FollowUpStatus.DONE
        if today > self.due_date:
            return FollowUpStatus.OVERDUE
        return self.status

def send_followup_digest(
    incident_id: str,
    items: list[FollowUpItem],
    review_date: date,
    as_of: date | None = None,
) -> dict:
    today = as_of or date.today()
    evaluated = [(item, item.evaluate(today)) for item in items]
    overdue = [item for item, status in evaluated if status == FollowUpStatus.OVERDUE]
    done = [item for item, status in evaluated if status == FollowUpStatus.DONE]
    pending = [item for item, status in evaluated
               if status in (FollowUpStatus.PENDING, FollowUpStatus.IN_PROGRESS)]
    return {
        "incident_id": incident_id,
        "days_since_review": (today - review_date).days,
        "total": len(items),
        "done": len(done),
        "pending": len(pending),
        "overdue": len(overdue),
        "overdue_items": [{"id": i.decision_id, "owner": i.owner} for i in overdue],
        "complete": len(overdue) == 0 and len(pending) == 0,
    }

The EM sends a digest to all exec review attendees at 7 days and 30 days after the meeting, or earlier if any item goes overdue. A 30-day follow-up rate of 100% closed is the target — any item still open at 30 days should have a recorded reason and a new due date, not just silence.
When the Review Reveals Systemic Issues

Some exec reviews surface problems that a single postmortem action item cannot fix: chronic understaffing of the on-call rotation, accumulated technical debt that makes every incident harder, or an org structure that creates cross-team coordination failures. These are systemic issues that require organisational decisions, not engineering fixes.

When a systemic issue is identified in the exec review, the decision record must capture it explicitly. The engineering team should not try to fix an organisational problem with a technical action item — that produces a nominal fix (a runbook update, an alert threshold change) while the underlying structural cause persists.

If the review shows that a team had more than three P0/P1 pages in a month, the exec review is the appropriate venue to discuss rotation size, on-call compensation, or temporary staffing relief — not a postmortem action item for the on-call engineer to figure out.

If the incident root cause involves a system with known debt and no owner, the exec review decision must include a named owner and a committed remediation timeline, or an explicit decision to accept the risk and document it.

If the incident was extended by a cross-team hand-off delay, the fix is not a runbook — it is a process agreement between teams, brokered at the exec level, about escalation paths and ownership boundaries.

The major incident executive review is a 60-minute structured meeting triggered by P0 incidents, high-impact P1s, repeat failures, and privacy or safety incidents. It is gated on a complete postmortem with assigned action items. The team prepares a business-level review package — not the technical postmortem — that translates incident details into customer impact, financial exposure, and an explicit list of decisions requested. The meeting produces a binding decision record with owners and due dates for each decision. For AI services, model safety incidents, capability regressions, and data exposure events require specialised attendees and may trigger external notification obligations. The most common failure mode is holding the meeting without a mechanism for follow-through: a decision record without enforcement is an expensive postmortem discussion with executive attendance.
